title: Mach's principle in atomic transitions
abstract: We investigate the atomic transition probabilities in atom-mirror set-ups that are in circular motion. In one scenario, the atom is in circular motion inside a static cylindrical mirror. In the other scenario, the cylindrical mirror rotates around its central axis while the atom remains static. We report structural similarity in the atomic transition probabilities between these two cases -- these probabilities are equivalent upon interchanging the field frequencies between the two scenarios. We interpret such an observation as a semi-classical phenomenon analogous to the classical Mach's principle.
summary: The paper theoretically examines atomic transitions near a cylindrical mirror under two different dynamic conditions: the atom moving or the mirror rotating. By calculating transition probabilities in both scenarios, the authors demonstrate a structural equivalence between the results when field frequencies are interchanged. This finding is presented as compelling evidence for a quantum mechanical manifestation of Mach's principle.
main_problem: The study investigates structural similarities in atomic transition probabilities when comparing two scenarios: an atom moving in a static cylindrical mirror versus a static atom near a rotating mirror.
main_result: The calculated transition probability rates show structural equivalence upon interchanging the field mode frequencies between the two non-inertial reference frames, suggesting a quantum analogue to Mach's principle.
method: Transition probabilities are calculated using perturbation theory (Dyson series) for an Unruh-DeWitt detector coupled to a scalar quantum field confined by a cylindrical boundary. The analysis involves comparing time-integrated and rate-defined quantities in both static and rotating coordinates.
model_or_system: The system models an atom interacting with a background real scalar quantum field ($\phi$) whose modes are constrained by a perfect reflecting cylindrical mirror. The comparison is drawn between the case where the atom undergoes circular motion inside a fixed mirror, and the case where the mirror rotates while the atom remains stationary.
key_observables: Atomic transition probabilities ($P_{ex}$) and their corresponding rates ($	ext{Re}_{ex}$ or $R_{ex}$).
important_parameters:
Field mode frequencies ($
u$), atomic energy gap ($\omega$), coupling strength ($g$), and the rotational frequency ($\Omega$) or radial position ($a$).
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to the lowest order of coupling strength ($g \ll 1$), and the definition of transition rates is necessary due to potential divergences over infinite time.
figures_summary: Figure 2 plots the ratio of calculated transition rates ($	ext{Rex}/g^2$) for both the moving-atom/static-mirror case and the static-atom/rotating-mirror case, demonstrating qualitative and quantitative agreement at small atomic radii ($a$).
paper_structure: The paper establishes the physical model using an Unruh-DeWitt detector coupled to a field confined by a cylinder. It then calculates transition probabilities for two analogous scenarios (moving atom vs. rotating mirror) in both time-integrated and rate forms, culminating in a comparison that highlights the structural equivalence.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses fundamental principles like Mach's principle using quantum electrodynamics/field theory in non-inertial frames, which is highly relevant to advanced AMO physics concerning vacuum structure and boundary effects.

figures.caption: FIG. 1: In the above figure, we have provided the schematic diagrams for the two different scenarios. On the left, the atom is in circular motion maintaining a radial distance a from the central axis, i.e., the Z−axis. At the same time, the mirror is a static cylinder of radius R0. On the right, the atom is static at a radial distance a from the central axis. In the second scenario, the mirror is cylindrical, with radius R0, and rotates about its central axis.

FIG. 2: In the above figure, we have plotted Rex(ω)/g2 and Rex(˜ω)/g2, that respectively represent the atomic transition probability rates corresponding to two different scenarios, with respect to the radial position of the atom a. In particular, on the left, we consider the atomic energy gaps to be ω = ˜ω. At the same time, on the right, we choose ˜ω = m Ω−ω/γ. We choose the maximum value in the numerical sum over n to be 20 and, over m, from −20 to 20, beyond which we do not observe any deviation from the obtained results.
